title: Human-in-the-Loop Tool Approval
concept: Requiring explicit user confirmation before a sensitive tool call executes.

exercises.prompt: Why did we establish in the last lesson that arbitrary tool access is dangerous?

options.text: Tools always execute perfectly because LLMs never make logical mistakes.
Blindly giving an LLM access to powerful tools like the system shell is highly risky.
All MCP tools are sandboxed automatically and can never harm your computer.

exercises.explain: Blindly trusting an LLM with powerful tools can lead to catastrophic errors, data loss, or unauthorized actions, which is why we must control permissioning.

cards.title: Who Has the Final Say?
cards.body: AI agents are incredibly fast, but they don't have common sense. If an AI misinterprets your prompt and decides to format your hard drive to 'make space,' who stops it? That's where you come in.

cards.title: What is Human-in-the-Loop?
cards.body: Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) tool approval is a security design pattern where an application intercepts a tool call generated by an AI, shows it to the user, and waits for explicit approval before running it. This prevents the model from executing dangerous actions without your knowledge.

cards.title: The Guard at the Gate
cards.body: Think of tool approval like notifications on your smartphone. When an app wants to access your camera or photos, it doesn't just take them; your phone pauses and pops up a prompt asking: "Allow App to Access Photos?" You are the loop that grants or denies that power.

cards.title: A Close Call Saved by HITL
cards.body: Imagine an AI coder wants to run 'rm -rf /' to clean up a folder. Instead of running it instantly, the MCP client displays: 'The AI wants to delete all files in your root directory. Approve or Deny?' You click 'Deny' and save your computer.

cards.title: Does HITL Slow Us Down Too Much?
cards.body: You might think adding human approval defeats the purpose of an autonomous AI. In reality, keeping a human in the loop builds trust, allowing you to deploy AI in high-stakes environments like finance and medical systems where raw automation is too risky.

cards.title: Auto-Pilot vs. Co-Pilot
cards.body: Compare an automated pipeline that can easily run amok versus a controlled pipeline that keeps you in command.

left.text: AI decides -> Tool runs instantly -> OOPS! Files deleted without warning.

right.text: AI decides -> App pauses -> User approves -> Tool runs safely.

exercises.prompt: What should a good Human-in-the-Loop approval dialog display to the user?

options.text: Just a green button that says 'Go' without any text.
The name of the tool and the exact arguments it wants to use.
A long list of system code that is impossible to read.
Nothing, the UI should be kept completely blank to avoid confusion.

exercises.explain: An approval UI must show the exact tool name and the specific arguments (like the path of the file being deleted) so the user can make an informed decision.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If an AI model is 99% confident in its decision, we should bypass the human approval step for deleting files.
exercises.explain: Even if the AI is highly confident, it can still hallucinate or be manipulated by malicious prompt injection. Critical actions must always be verified by a human.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe how human approval keeps tool execution safe.
exercises.sentence: The application intercepts the tool call and waits for user confirmation before executing it.
exercises.explain: The security layer must intercept the call before the server runs it, giving the user a chance to review and cancel the action.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following scenarios demonstrates an AI application using Human-in-the-Loop tool approval?

options.text: AI: 'I have deleted your inbox files to save space as requested.'
Client UI: 'Tool Call: send_wire_transfer(amount=1000, recipient="Bob"). Click [Approve] or [Reject] to proceed.'
User: 'Please check the current weather in New York using the weather tool.'

exercises.explain: The second option shows an AI agent attempting to execute a sensitive action (transferring money) but responsibly holding execution until a human confirms. The others are either fully automated or not using tool approval.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following tool calls should ABSOLUTELY require a Human-in-the-Loop approval step before executing?

options.text: A tool that fetches the current time in London
A tool that searches Google for public facts
A tool that deletes a user account from a database
A tool that calculates the square root of a number

exercises.explain: For maximum safety, write operations (like deleting databases, sending emails, or transferring money) should always require human confirmation. Read-only operations, like checking the weather or reading public documentation, are safe to run automatically.
